title: The Python import package must be named pptx_cli, never pptx
rationale: python-pptx already uses the import namespace pptx. Reusing that module name would create a collision that breaks both packages at import time.
guidance: Always use pptx_cli as the Python package name in imports, project layout, and tooling configuration. Never create a top-level pptx package in this repository.
consequences: Import collision with python-pptx, broken runtime for both this tool and any code using python-pptx.

title: The public CLI executable name must remain pptx
rationale: The CLI is designed for both human and agent use under a short, memorable command name. Changing it would break all documented workflows, scripts, and agent integrations.
guidance: The console_scripts entry must map pptx to the CLI entrypoint. Do not rename the executable.
consequences: Breaking change for all users, scripts, CI pipelines, and agent integrations that invoke pptx.

title: Every command must return a single structured JSON envelope in machine-readable mode
rationale: Agents parse one schema, not N. A single envelope shape for every command eliminates the need for multiple parsers and format guessing.
guidance: Every command, success or failure, must return a JSON object with schema_version, request_id, ok, command, result, errors, warnings, and metrics. On failure, result is null, never omitted.
consequences: Agents cannot reliably parse CLI output. Multiple parsers needed. Agent automation becomes fragile.

title: Error codes must be stable, prefixed by category, and never renamed or removed
rationale: Agents branch on error codes, not messages. Renaming or removing codes breaks all downstream automation silently.
guidance: Use the ERR_ prefix taxonomy (ERR_VALIDATION_*, ERR_POLICY_*, ERR_CONFLICT_*, ERR_IO_*, ERR_INTERNAL_*). Add new codes freely but never rename or remove existing ones.
consequences: Agent automation silently breaks when error codes change. CI pipelines fail to distinguish error categories.

title: Exit codes must use distinct numeric ranges per error category
rationale: Shell scripts and CI pipelines branch on exit codes. Using exit 1 for everything makes it impossible to distinguish retry-worthy errors from input errors from bugs.
guidance: Use: 0=success, 10=validation error, 20=permission/policy failure, 40=conflict/stale state, 50=I/O failure, 90=internal error. One error category per exit code range.
consequences: CI pipelines and shell scripts cannot distinguish error categories without parsing JSON.

title: Manifest extracted facts and human annotations must be stored in separate files
rationale: Mixing extracted machine facts with human annotations causes drift. Separate files ensure extracted facts remain deterministic and reproducible while annotations can evolve independently.
guidance: Store extracted facts in manifest.yaml and human annotations in annotations.yaml. Never mutate manifest.yaml with human-authored content.
consequences: Human edits corrupt machine-extracted facts. Re-extraction overwrites human annotations. Determinism is lost.

title: Mutating commands must support --dry-run with structured change summaries
rationale: Agents plan-then-execute. Dry-run is the plan phase. Without it, agents must guess or mutate blindly.
guidance: Every command that creates or modifies on-disk artifacts must accept --dry-run. Dry-run must execute the full pipeline, return what would change as structured data, and write nothing.
consequences: Agents cannot preview mutations. No safe plan-then-execute workflow. Blind mutations risk data loss.

title: stdout must be reserved for structured data in machine-readable mode
rationale: Every decorative line printed to stdout is a token the agent pays for and must discard. Mixing progress or banners with data forces agents to maintain multiple parsers.
guidance: stdout is exclusively for the structured JSON response. stderr is for progress, debug logs, banners, and diagnostics. Never print update notifications, tips, or decorative output to stdout.
consequences: Agent token budgets wasted on noise. Parsing becomes fragile. Machine-readable mode is broken.

title: File writes must use safe atomic-write semantics
rationale: A crash mid-write leaves the original file intact only if writes use atomic semantics. Without this, agents can corrupt data and not know it.
guidance: Write to a temporary file in the same directory, fsync, then atomic-rename. Clean up on failure. Never write directly to the target file.
consequences: Crash mid-write corrupts target files. Data loss is silent and unrecoverable.

title: Input .pptx, YAML, and JSON files must be treated as untrusted
rationale: Input files may come from external sources and could contain malicious constructs such as zip bombs, path traversal, or oversized packages.
guidance: Validate paths and archive members before extraction. Avoid writing outside explicit output directories. Handle malicious .pptx package parsing, zip bombs, path traversal, and malformed manifest/spec input.
consequences: Path traversal, zip bomb extraction, command injection, or unintended file writes from malicious input.

title: Command modules must be thin orchestrators that delegate to core/
rationale: Keeping command modules thin ensures reusable logic lives in core/ where it can be tested independently and shared across commands.
guidance: Command files in src/pptx_cli/commands/ should contain only orchestration logic. Move reusable business logic, runtime behavior, and shared services into src/pptx_cli/core/.
exceptions: Trivial read-only commands like guide may inline simple logic.
consequences: Business logic trapped in command modules cannot be reused or tested independently. Duplication creeps in.

title: Shared contracts must use typed Pydantic models, not ad hoc dictionaries
rationale: Typed models provide schema validation, serialization, and documentation. Ad hoc dictionaries leak untyped data across module boundaries.
guidance: Define shared data contracts as Pydantic BaseModel subclasses in src/pptx_cli/models/. Use typed models for contracts before introducing ad hoc dictionaries that leak across modules.
consequences: Untyped dictionaries leak across modules, causing runtime type errors, missing field bugs, and loss of schema-generation capabilities.

title: Composition must clone approved template structures, not recreate them from styling rules
rationale: Recreating design from abstract style tokens causes drift from the original template. Cloning preserves exact enterprise fidelity.
guidance: The composition engine must clone approved layout structures and dependent assets from the original template. Never regenerate design from abstract styling rules alone.
consequences: Generated slides drift from the corporate template. Visual fidelity is lost. The core product thesis is violated.

title: Only approved layouts and declared placeholders may be used during composition
rationale: Using unapproved layouts or undeclared placeholders breaks the template contract and produces slides that violate corporate identity rules.
guidance: The composition engine must validate that only approved layouts are used and only declared placeholders are filled. Content types must match placeholder types.
consequences: Slides use wrong layouts or fill undeclared placeholders. Template contract is violated. Corporate identity is compromised.

title: CLI command names and envelope fields must not change without synchronized doc and test updates
rationale: The CLI contract is consumed by agents, scripts, and CI pipelines. Changing it without updating docs and tests causes silent breakage and inconsistency.
guidance: Do not change CLI command names, response envelope fields, error-code taxonomy, or exit-code mapping without updating docs and tests in the same change.
consequences: Docs, tests, and implementation diverge. Agents and users encounter undocumented behavior changes.

title: The guide command must expose a complete machine-readable CLI schema
rationale: Agents should not need external docs. A machine-readable guide command enables zero-shot CLI usage by returning every command, flag, error code, and example as structured data.
guidance: The pptx guide command must return the CLI command catalog, input and output schema references, error code taxonomy, exit-code mapping, identifier conventions, and representative examples.
consequences: Agents must discover the CLI by trial and error, burning tokens and API calls probing for valid invocations.

title: Given identical inputs, output must be deterministic and reproducible
rationale: Determinism enables caching, diffing, and trust. Non-deterministic output makes validation, CI, and agent workflows unreliable.
guidance: Given the same manifest, content payload, and CLI version, output should be reproducible. Avoid non-deterministic operations like random IDs or timestamp-dependent formatting in generated artifacts.
exceptions: request_id and timestamps in the response envelope are inherently non-deterministic but are metadata, not content.
consequences: CI validation becomes flaky. Agents cannot cache or diff outputs reliably. Trust in generated artifacts is undermined.

title: New behavior must ship with tests covering happy path and failure path
rationale: Untested behavior is unverified behavior. The CLI contract depends on tests to catch regressions in envelope shape, error codes, and exit codes.
guidance: Every PR must test: happy path for the changed behavior, failure path with expected error code or validation response, machine-readable output shape if command behavior changes, and documentation updates when user-facing command semantics change.
consequences: Regressions in envelope shape, error codes, or exit codes go undetected. Contract stability is undermined.

title: The PyPI distribution name must remain pptx-cli
rationale: The distribution name is the public package identifier on PyPI. Changing it would break pip install instructions and downstream dependency specifications.
guidance: Keep the PyPI distribution name as pptx-cli unless an explicit migration plan is documented.
exceptions: May be changed with an explicit migration plan documented in an ADR.
consequences: Breaking change for all pip install instructions and downstream dependency specifications.

title: The tool must never silently degrade template fidelity
rationale: Silent fidelity degradation undermines the core product thesis. Users and agents must know when exact preservation is not possible.
guidance: When exact preservation is not possible, warn or fail according to policy. Default to warn mode in v1 with --strict available for CI and governance workflows.
consequences: Users unknowingly distribute slides that violate corporate identity. Trust in the tool is lost.

title: Wrapper generation must remain thin and delegate to the shared engine
rationale: Generated wrappers should be minimal scaffolds that delegate to the core engine. Thick wrappers create maintenance burden and diverge from the primary CLI behavior.
guidance: The wrapper generator emits a thin Python package scaffold that exposes template-specific commands and delegates to the shared core engine. Do not duplicate core logic in generated wrappers.
consequences: Generated wrappers diverge from the primary CLI. Bugs are fixed in one but not the other. Maintenance burden multiplies.

title: Package version must have a single source of truth
rationale: Multiple version declarations drift. A single source of truth ensures version bumps are atomic and consistent across the package, CLI output, and published metadata.
guidance: Define the version in one canonical location and reference it everywhere else. Support easy semantic-version bumps.
consequences: Version numbers diverge between pyproject.toml, CLI output, and published metadata. Users see inconsistent version information.

title: Manifest schema semantics must not change without an explicit migration path
rationale: Agents and wrappers depend on manifest schema stability. Changing semantics without a migration path silently breaks downstream consumers.
guidance: Do not change manifest schema semantics casually. When changes are necessary, provide an explicit migration path and document it. Additive changes are preferred over breaking changes.
consequences: Existing manifests become incompatible. Agents and wrappers break silently. Users must manually fix manifests without guidance.

title: When implementation details are unclear, prefer TODOs over speculative product decisions
rationale: Speculative decisions made during implementation often conflict with actual product requirements. Guided TODOs preserve optionality and make assumptions visible.
guidance: If behavior would create a new product decision not in PRD.md, a schema or CLI contract change would be speculative, or a fixture assumption cannot be verified, stop and leave a guided TODO marker.
consequences: Speculative decisions become embedded in code and are hard to reverse. Product requirements and implementation diverge silently.

title: cli.py must own only routing, envelope emission, and exit-code mapping
rationale: cli.py is the boundary between the CLI framework and the application. Keeping it focused on routing and envelope emission prevents it from becoming a monolith.
guidance: cli.py owns command composition, top-level routing, response envelope generation, error-code and exit-code mapping, and output-mode handling. Business logic belongs in commands/ and core/.
consequences: cli.py becomes a monolith mixing routing with business logic. Testing becomes difficult. Separation of concerns is lost.
